summary.assessment: Task 6 implements D25 (scheme-whitelist) and D17 (hardcoded asset path) correctly. SafeUriHandler blocks all non-http/https schemes including the classic Android URI injection vectors (intent://, javascript:, file:, content:, data:, sms:, tel:, market:, malformed) in fail-safe mode (silent return, not throw). AboutScreen reads a compile-time-constant asset name, so path traversal via user/state input is not reachable. Intent is constructed with ACTION_VIEW + FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK, no extras, no package targeting — no redirection risk. No hardcoded secrets. Zero critical or major findings. Four minor findings are defense-in-depth hardening suggestions: (1) scheme comparison is case-sensitive so legitimate uppercase/mixed-case schemes (e.g. `HTTP://`, `HTTPS://`) are silently blocked — a correctness issue with a fail-safe outcome but worth normalising; (2) test enumeration is the exact 11 TAC-13 anchors but does not exercise known Android-specific attack vectors (case variation, leading whitespace, embedded null byte, nested `intent://#Intent;scheme=http;end`, percent-encoded scheme) that would strengthen confidence; (3) compose-richtext 1.0.0-alpha02 is an alpha dependency parsing LLM-generated markdown — no public CVE found, but an alpha version on a markdown parser deserves an explicit upgrade watch; (4) `runCatching { context.startActivity(intent) }` silently swallows ActivityNotFoundException for *all* cases including legitimate http/https — acceptable per D25 but forensically opaque.

findings.title: Scheme comparison is case-sensitive; legitimate uppercase/mixed-case http(s) links are silently blocked
findings.description: `ALLOWED_SCHEMES = setOf("http", "https")` is compared against `Uri.parse(uri).scheme` verbatim. Android's `Uri.getScheme()` returns the scheme in the exact case it was written — it does NOT lower-case-normalise. Per RFC 3986 §3.1 schemes are case-insensitive, and every real-world browser treats `HTTP://example.com` as valid http. The current implementation silently drops markdown links like `[foo](HTTP://bar)` or `[foo](Https://bar)` because `"HTTP" !in setOf("http","https")`. Fail-safe for security (no bypass: `JavaScript:alert(1)` scheme = `"JavaScript"`, still not whitelisted, still blocked) — but a correctness / availability issue that mis-categorises legitimate traffic as hostile. Also prevents any future test like `HTTP_allowed` from passing without code change.
findings.impact: Legitimate links in LLM-rendered markdown or in `about.md` with uppercase schemes are silently dropped with no user feedback. Fail-safe direction: no security bypass (blocked schemes stay blocked regardless of case, because the whitelist itself is lowercase-only). Pure availability/UX issue.
findings.recommendation:
Normalise before comparing. Minimal diff:
```kotlin
val scheme = runCatching { Uri.parse(uri).scheme?.lowercase() }.getOrNull() ?: return
if (scheme !in ALLOWED_SCHEMES) return
```
Add a regression test `http_uppercase_allowed` → `HTTP://example.com` should dispatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Lowercase the parsed scheme before the set-membership check. Keep ALLOWED_SCHEMES itself lowercase. Verify with a new `HTTP_allowed` test and a `JavaScript_blocked` test to confirm case-insensitive whitelist does not accidentally widen the blocked surface.

findings.title: TAC-13 test enumeration does not exercise Android-specific URI-injection edge cases
findings.description: The 11 tests map 1:1 to the TAC-13 anchors from the task spec (2 allowed + 9 blocked scheme categories) and are sufficient for task acceptance. However, they do not cover recognised URI-injection variations that have been used historically to bypass scheme-whitelists on Android: (a) case-variation of known-bad schemes — `JavaScript:alert(1)`, `Intent://...`, `JAVASCRIPT:...` (happens to be blocked *because* the whitelist is lowercase-only — see minor-1 — but the coupling is accidental, not asserted); (b) leading/embedded whitespace — `" http://"`, `"http://\u0000foo"`; (c) nested intent-scheme — `intent://example.com#Intent;scheme=http;package=com.evil;end` (common Android exploit where parser sees scheme=http after normalisation); (d) percent-encoded scheme — `%68ttp://example.com` (URIs must not percent-encode the scheme, but implementations vary); (e) fragment/query-only strings — `"#javascript:alert(1)"`, `"?scheme=http"`. Current implementation (Uri.parse + .scheme) is expected to handle all of these fail-safe, but tests would lock that in against a future refactor (e.g. manual string-prefix parsing).
findings.impact: Audit coverage is narrower than threat surface. A future refactor that replaces `Uri.parse(uri).scheme` with `uri.substringBefore(":")` would silently pass the current 11 tests but break on `" http://evil"` (leading space ⇒ scheme = `" http"`) or case variants. Defense-in-depth gap, not a present vulnerability.
findings.cwe: CWE-1287 (Improper Validation of Specified Type of Input)
findings.suggested_fix: Add the six tests above to SafeUriHandlerTest. Keep them alongside the existing 11 — they lock down behaviour that is currently implicit (and would silently regress under a future rewrite).

findings.title: compose-richtext 1.0.0-alpha02 is an alpha markdown parser; no CVE disclosed, but alpha on security-relevant path
findings.description:
`com.halilibo.compose-richtext:richtext-commonmark:1.0.0-alpha02` and `richtext-ui-material3:1.0.0-alpha02` are alpha releases that parse LLM-generated markdown — the same content that SafeMarkdown exists to sanitise. Alpha markers on CommonMark parsers have historically shipped with input-handling bugs (incomplete-tag unbalanced-stack crashes, XSS-style injection via nested autolinks, etc.). At audit time no CVE is disclosed for this artifact, but:
1. The `-alpha02` qualifier is itself a signal — no public security review / fuzzing against CommonMark pathological inputs is known for this version.
2. The parser directly consumes model output that can be adversarial (jailbroken prompts, indirect prompt injection from pasted context).
3. Upstream (halilibo/compose-richtext) is maintained primarily by one author with limited release cadence.
This is explicitly accepted in tech-spec D8, so not a blocker for Task 6. Flagged because this is the natural audit checkpoint.
findings.impact: If the markdown parser contains a crash or infinite-loop bug on a crafted LLM-emitted markdown fragment, the About screen would render fine (static content), but downstream consumers of `SafeMarkdown` in MessageBubble / ThinkingBlock (per tech-spec) could DoS the chat UI. No memory-safety concerns (Kotlin/JVM). No known RCE surface.
findings.recommendation: Non-blocking for this task. For Phase 2 as a whole, add a tracking item to re-evaluate the pin when a non-alpha release ships (check https://github.com/halilibo/compose-richtext/releases). In the interim, consider a single defensive fuzz-test that feeds pathological markdown (deeply nested emphasis, unclosed code fences, enormous tables) into `SafeMarkdown` under Robolectric — not to assert correctness of rendering but to assert it does not throw uncaught. This task's author should simply note the dependency posture in Post-completion decisions.md; no code change required here.
findings.cwe: CWE-1104 (Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components)
findings.suggested_fix: Document the alpha-dependency posture in work/phase-2-ui/decisions.md Task 6 entry (one line: 'compose-richtext alpha retained per D8; re-evaluate on non-alpha release'). Optionally add a smoke test that feeds a large pathological-markdown fixture through SafeMarkdown under Robolectric to detect parser regressions on dependency bumps.

findings.owasp: A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: Blocked-scheme and startActivity failure paths are indistinguishable — no audit trail for potentially hostile input
findings.description: Both the blocked-scheme branch (`if (scheme !in ALLOWED_SCHEMES) return`) and the exception-swallow branch (`runCatching { context.startActivity(intent) }`) produce the same observable outcome: nothing happens. There is no `ErrorLog.e(...)` call, no telemetry, no breadcrumb. Tech-spec D25 explicitly calls this out as 'silently ignore — blocked scheme is expected UX, not an error (no ErrorLog entry)', and that IS the right default for normal markdown (users should not be error-dialogued when a phone-number link is tapped). However, from a security-monitoring standpoint, an attacker-controlled LLM output that emits `[click me](intent://com.target/launcher#Intent;...)` will be silently dropped without any forensic trace. Patterns.md already has an `ErrorLog` whitelist; tech-spec D27 adds new whitelist entries but does not include a markdown-link bucket.
findings.impact: Operationally low — the handler blocks the hostile URI successfully (defence works), but if an attack is ever reported by a user, there is no log to reconstruct what the LLM emitted. Also makes diagnosis of 'my http link silently failed' user reports harder. Not a security hole — a logging/telemetry gap.
findings.recommendation: Accept for this task per D25. For future hardening, consider a rate-limited debug-only log (not ErrorLog, since D25 explicitly excludes it) at `Log.d("SafeUriHandler", "blocked scheme: $scheme")` behind `BuildConfig.DEBUG`. For release builds, retain silent behaviour. Do not expose the full URI in the log — only the scheme — to avoid leaking PII embedded in query params. If ErrorLog integration is later desired, coordinate with D27 to add a new `"markdown-link"` whitelist entry.
findings.suggested_fix: Optional: add a `if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) Log.d(TAG, "blocked scheme: $scheme")` inside the non-whitelisted branch. Do NOT log the full URI. Do NOT wire to ErrorLog — that contradicts D25. This is a defence-in-depth observability improvement, not a correctness fix.

positive_findings.note: D25 correctly implemented as defense-in-depth: `Uri.parse` is wrapped in `runCatching` (returns null instead of throwing on any pathological input), scheme is checked against a lowercase whitelist, and `startActivity` is also wrapped in `runCatching` (catches ActivityNotFoundException when no browser is installed). All four classes of URI-injection covered by the task spec (intent-scheme, smsto/tel, javascript, file/content/data/market) fail-safe to a silent no-op.

D17 path-traversal protection is honored structurally, not just in policy: `private const val ABOUT_ASSET = "about.md"` is a compile-time constant with an explicit inline comment referencing the decision. The value is never derived from state, Intent extras, navigation arguments, or any user input. `context.assets.open` is constrained to the APK's asset container, so even if the constant were mutable, filesystem traversal outside the APK is not reachable.

No injection surface: AboutScreen reads a bundled static asset, there are no DB queries, no HTTP calls, no shell exec, no dynamic code loading (no reflection, no `Class.forName`, no `loadLibrary` from user input). The only dynamic input crossing a trust boundary is the URI passed to SafeUriHandler, which is sanitised.

Intent is constructed with `Intent(ACTION_VIEW, parsed)` — no extras, no `setComponent`, no `setPackage`, no `setClassName`. This means the system resolves to the user's default browser via the standard ACTION_VIEW intent filter. No intent-redirection / component-hijack risk. `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` is correctly set for non-Activity contexts (required when `LocalContext` is ever an ApplicationContext, e.g. during tests).

N/A — About screen is not auth-gated, has no session handling, holds no credentials. Confirmed no hardcoded secrets anywhere in the diff (grepped ABOUT_ASSET, about.md, SafeMarkdown, strings.xml, SanctumApp, ModelManagerScreen).
N/A — no cryptographic primitives, no secret storage, no TLS configuration added. Feature surface is purely presentational.

`about.md` contains only project-descriptive text and public attribution links (github.com/google-ai-edge, ai.google.dev). No PII, no device identifiers, no analytics IDs. SafeMarkdown is consistently applied: AboutScreen is the only current call site and it wraps the full asset content — the markdown rendering cannot leak to raw WebView or any other unsandboxed renderer.

N/A — SafeUriHandler does not make server-side requests. It hands the URI to the OS via `startActivity(ACTION_VIEW)`, which dispatches to the user-configured browser in the user's own security context. No fetch/HTTP-client is invoked from this code path.

dependency_scan.known_cves: None disclosed at audit time (2026-04-16) for these artifacts/versions.
dependency_scan.notes: Alpha versions on a markdown parser consuming LLM output — see minor-3. No test dependency added beyond JUnit 4 / Robolectric 4.12 / androidx.test:core which are already vetted via Task 3/4.

out_of_scope_confirmation.note: Per task prompt, pre-existing permissions, manifest privacy hardening (Task 5 territory), and general project structure were not re-audited. The diff is scoped to the 7 files enumerated in the task prompt.
